data.title: GitLab: Share Price Erosion Has Little To Do With Strong Fundamentals
data.description:
Amid a sky-high stock market as we enter 2026, investors have a conundrum to face in the markets: AI is the primary catalyst that has driven gains this year. It’s a relatively new force in revolutionizing the workplace and replacing processes, and yet, the stock market has already made rather clear delineations of winners and losers, which may be quite premature.

GitLab (GTLB), a DevSecOps software company that caters to software developers, has been marked a clear loser here. Despite strong growth amid a sales leadership shuffle, shares of GitLab have lost 30% of their value this year. Is this a warning signal to run for the hills or an opportunity for long-term investors to get in at a discount?

I last wrote a "Strong Buy" article on GitLab in September, when the stock was trading in the mid-$40s. Since then, new sales leadership has begun to fill out the ranks at GitLab, seemingly without any disruption, as growth has continued at a healthy mid-20% pace. It’s becoming more clear here that fears over AI are overblown, and as such, GitLab is still a "Buy" (though I'm moderating my rating down slightly due to the federal/macro headwinds that presented themselves this quarter).

As a reminder for investors who are newer to GitLab, here are all the bull case arguments in GitLab’s favor:

  • AI boosts coding speed, but not quality. While the job of software developers is in the rapid process of shifting from writing to reviewing code, GitLab is of the core belief that companies still need to coordinate to ensure the products they ship are high quality. This ensures the criticality of a DevOps tool like GitLab, and growth rates suggest that AI is not, in fact, an existential threat for the company. GitLab believes AI has expanded, not shrunk, its TAM.
  • Potential to clear backlog. The company notes that growth progress was recently impacted by the U.S. federal government shutdown, while overall win rates and pipeline development were unimpacted. This could have the impact of pushing more business out and providing an unexpected growth kick.
  • International growth push. Among the company’s priorities in reshuffling its sales leadership is to chase further international growth opportunities. The company recently onboarded a new global sales head, reporting to the CRO and tasked with international expansion.
  • Operating with Rule of 40 principles in mind. The company is achieving mid-20% revenue growth coupled with high-teens operating margins, positioning it comfortably within the Rule of 40. This contrasts starkly against the “growth at all costs” nature of the most popular AI plays, and investors may come to appreciate GitLab’s more conservative operating mindset amid a potential risk-off heading into next year.
  • Clean balance sheet. Again, other AI plays like Oracle have raised eyebrows recently at boosting debt loads to finance data center buildouts. GitLab stands apart from the pack with its debt-free balance sheet, ongoing FCF, and more than $1 billion in net cash, which is a substantial chunk of the stock’s current market value.

Meanwhile, undeniably the top reason to like GitLab now is an incredibly attractive valuation. At current share prices near $40, GitLab trades at a market cap of $6.86 billion. Netting off the $1.20 billion of cash off GitLab’s most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $5.66 billion.

For the remainder of FY26 (the year for GitLab ending in January 2026), the company has guided to $946-$947 million in revenue, or 25% y/y growth, which is higher than $936-$942 million (23%-24% previously).

I had mentioned last quarter that GitLab’s prior outlook baked in conservatism for the company’s new chief revenue officer, who was tasked with additional mid-market and enterprise expansion; the upward guidance trend suggests these efforts are playing out well. And for next year, FY27, Wall Street is expecting $1.23 billion in revenue, or a further 20% y/y growth. This positions GitLab’s valuation multiples at:

  • 6.0x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 4.6x EV/FY27 revenue

This is an inexplicably low valuation for a Rule of 40 software company growing revenue north of >20% y/y. When we stack up GitLab against other software peers growing in the mid-20s, GitLab trades at roughly half of peers like ServiceNow (NOW). But the most stark comparison is against JFrog, a company that competes directly against GitLab in the DevSecOps space (and as such, if GitLab faces any risk at all from AI, JFrog is in the same boat). JFrog’s revenue grew at the same 25% y/y pace in the most recent quarter, while the Street is also expecting ~20% growth next year. Despite their business and financial similarities, JFrog stock has doubled this year while GitLab lagged, leaving the former at a ~14x forward revenue multiple that trounces GitLab.

Take advantage of this nonsensical dip in this attractive “growth at a reasonable price” stock to buy GitLab with confidence and wait ahead of a possible upward re-rating.

Q3 download

Let’s now go through GitLab’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

GitLab’s revenue grew 25% y/y to $244.4 million, handily beating Wall Street’s expectations of $239.5 million (+23% y/y) by a two-point margin. Growth did slow from 29% growth in Q2, but not nearly by as much as the company’s original guidance calling for 22-23% growth in the quarter had suggested. We think that for the current Q4, investors are falling into the same possible trap. Guidance calling for $251-$252 million in revenue next quarter implies a deceleration to 19% growth, though GitLab has a track record for meaningfully outperforming its very conservative bias for guidance.

This is especially true as GitLab had headwinds out of its control this quarter. The company notes that overall customer usage and deployment are healthy, leading to a vibrant pipeline for the company. However, its sizable federal sector segment faced challenging new business close rates thanks to the government shutdown.

From helpful anecdotal remarks made on the Q3 earnings call by CEO Bill Staples:

In the first 10 months of 2025, key activity metrics CI pipelines, deployments and releases are up about 35% to 45% year-over-year, similar to what peers are seeing. For customers paying us more than $5,000 in ARR, usage proxies like deployments and CI pipelines on a per seat basis are up 20% to 40% annually. Simply put, more code means more of a need for GitLab. Our 2025 Global DevSecOps report shows that while AI accelerates coding, more code doesn't necessarily mean better outcomes […]

Overall, sales cycles and win rates remain consistent. However, softness in the U.S. public sector offset part of that performance. GitLab continues to be viewed as the preferred software factory and trusted partner to leading U.S. agencies, but slower decision-making related to the subsequent government shutdown created some new headwinds in the quarter […]

We are expanding our go-to-market capacity and have hired a new business leader to build out our global first order team with a focus on acquiring new logos. It's important to note that resourcing and ramping up this team will take time, but I believe the payoff will be worth the investment. Every new customer we win today matters given their lifetime value. We are operating with urgency.”

We’re hopeful that delayed government deals will clear the backlog and provide an unexpected boost to revenue in the coming quarters. We think as well that a new, recharged senior sales leadership team should be able to drive new logos.

Where we do want to express caution is around GitLab’s historic exposure to smaller, mom-and-pop-type developers in a tough macro climate that has seen higher SMB failures amid inflating costs and weakening consumer demand. No doubt this weakness may be contributing to top-line deceleration pressure. That said, we do appreciate the fact that GitLab is growing its share of enterprise customers, which can stabilize overall revenue. The company added 61 net new customers, generating over $100k in ARR, ending Q3 with 1,405 such customers, up 23% y/y.

On the profitability front, despite recent investments into sales leadership hires, the company has cut spending on sales and marketing by 4 points as a percentage of revenue to 36%. G&A and R&D costs also fell by 1 and 2 points of revenue, respectively.

As a result, as shown in the chart above, GitLab’s pro forma operating margins leaped 5 points y/y to 18%. This positions GitLab’s rule of 40 score at a healthy 43. Against, we have to note the direct comparison against JFrog here, with the latter generating virtually identical metrics in Q3 at 25% revenue growth and 19% pro forma operating margins—despite the sharp valuation premium to GitLab.

Key takeaways

In my view, long-term investors have a fantastic opportunity here to look past near-term federal government and SMB headwinds to build a long position in GitLab at half of where similar software companies are trading. Don’t rule this company out as an AI loser yet when its growth rates are still clinging to an impressive mid-20% pace (despite hitting a >$1 billion annualized revenue run rate). Use this dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Lattice Semiconductor: The Rally Is Getting Ahead Of The Fundamentals
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Lattice Semiconductor Corporation (LSCC) is successfully transitioning into Edge AI and Communication & Computing markets that should accelerate future growth. Mid-range FPGAs (Field Programmable Gate Array) products such as Lattice Avant-G and Avant-X are gaining ground, capturing market share, and increasing the average selling price.

The market welcomed (+4% share price gain) the voted $250 million share buyback on 5 December, just after the CEO bought $1.9 million worth of shares in November, reinforcing investor confidence.

Q3 2025 results were mixed, showing positive signs of recovery after a difficult 2024 impacted by automotive markets and high inventories. Even if Q4 2025 guidance is better, I am still concerned with: (i) revenue slowdown from 23-24% in 2024 to +4.9% in Q3 2025 YoY and (ii) margin compression. Lattice published an operating profit loss in Q3, and net profit margins decreased 2-4% in 2025 from 27-30% in 2023.

The company trades at a 2025 P/E ratio of 74.7x due to depressed earnings from the 38-44x range in early 2024. On balance, I think that LSCC's share price went ahead of itself, pricing in already all the good news, which ultimately leads to a HOLD rating from my perspective. If net profit margins recover towards the historical 23-30% level with consistent revenue growth, valuation would appear more reasonable.

What does the company actually do?

Lattice competes in the low-power FPGA (Field Programmable Gate Array) segment with its proprietary designed software. Unlike competitors who target high-performance FPGAs for data centers, Lattice is focused on Edge Computing and low-powered applications. What it means is that the company’s devices consume little power and are very small; some are as small as 1.4mm x 1.4mm, making them suitable for smartphones, IoT, drones, or any wearable device. Additionally, Lattice emphasizes hardware security with Root-of-Trust and Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) features that allow it to protect the sensitive cryptographic assets against any potential quantum attack.

These are essential components for the edge of the network in areas such as Communication & computing: all technologies related to 5G, servers, and data centers. Industrial and Automotive, where Lattice is involved in IoT, ADAS, robotics, and finally consumer electronics (Dell’s XPS and Latitude laptops mainly). Based on the above, I include the table here detailing the “Solution Stack” offered by Lattice.

Revenue breakdown by geography and end product is shown in the graphs below from the most recent Q3 2025 results. Interestingly enough, Communication and Computing is now representing 55% of the total revenue, up from 48% in the same period last year, confirming the company’s efforts to pivot to high-growth segments.

Lattice's business model is to outsource manufacturing to TSMC, Samsung, and UMC, which gives them the “fabless” flexibility but ultimately makes them vulnerable to supply chain disruptions.

From the financial viewpoint, Lattice today has a $10.7 billion market cap and a $10.6 billion enterprise value, which implies for the year 2025 a 20.2x EV/revenue, 58.1x EBITDA, and 74.7x P/E multiples. More detailed information on the equity-to-enterprise-value bridge is presented below.

The Share Price Evolution and Analyst Ratings

Lattice's share price recovered in a “V” shaped manner from the April 2025 lows of $37, representing a rally of +111% at the latest share price of $77.95. Currently, Lattice sits just shy of its 52-week high of $81.23; that will be the ultimate test. For the time being, the chart below screams a “momentum buy,” catching up with its semiconductor benchmark.

Underperformance earlier this year can be attributed to Lattice’s focus on Asian markets (65% of total revenue) and weakness in automotive demand.

Most Recent Quarterly Performance

Looking at the most recent Q3 2025 results presented in the table below, in essence, they are showing the first sign of a recovery. Revenue grew +7.6% QoQ, mainly thanks to the Communication & Computing segment being a strong performer. EBITDA and EBIT margins at 35.6% and 29%, respectively, look very healthy. Free cash flow also increased from $31.3 million to $34 million, representing a 25.2% margin.

The major focus for investors for this quarter was on inventories. On that front, Lattice has shown some progress, as days inventory outstanding decreased to 193 days in September 2025 from 242 days a year earlier. Such normalization is a net positive for the working capital of the company and ultimately the cash flow.

Taking a step back, these numbers are stabilizing but did not recover just yet back to their 2023 levels. What can be of concern is that Q3 2025 YoY growth is only +4.9%, compared to 23-24% previously; the operating margin turned negative in Q3 2025 (-0.4%), and net profit margins also decreased to 2-4% in 2025 from 27-30% in 2023. The slide from the Q3 2025 report illustrates perfectly the fact that operational recovery is only in its initial phase.

For the Q4 2025 revenue outlook, the management guides us towards the $138-148 million range, which represents a median 22% growth with a 69.5% gross margin. Operating expenses are projected to be around $54.5-56.5 million. For the EPS, the company tables for $0.3-0.34 per share. On the company level, the Communications and Computing segment will drive future growth, while on the macro level, Industrial and Automotive rebound should also help.

Financial Projections

On the basis of management's Q4 2025 guidance, I assume $525 million revenue in 2025 with a 34.7% EBITDA margin. Given the fact that Lattice is successfully transitioning into Edge AI and Communication & Computing markets, I envisage a strong recovery in 2026 with revenues growing at +20.1%. Growth in 2027 is expected to be at +16.9%, as I believe Lattice Avant-G and Avant-X products with higher prices would support the top line. More details about the forecasts can be found in the table below.

The Current Valuation Provides …

For the valuation exercise, I selected from the table below a few peers that I find relevant for Lattice's trading multiple comparisons. All the companies below are involved in low-power Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) and related software solutions and compete directly on the same end markets: Industrial, Automotive, Communications, Computing and Consumer. However, only QuickLogic and Advanced Micro Devices have the fabless business model similar to Lattice. Microchip Technology and Texas Instruments are vertically integrated, while Analog Devices has a hybrid model with flexible capacity.

Looking at the trading multiples from the table below, we can see that Lattice is overvalued on every metric: +36.9% above the median forward P/E multiple and +54.0% above the EV/EBITDA multiple.

Now, from these valuation levels, is it possible to make a good return still? If we assume a free cash flow margin of 23.4% for FY2025, and we project +250 bps improvements each year, targeting a 33.4% free cash flow margin by the end of 2029, the analysis supports the conclusion that with a 35.5x P/FCF multiple, there is no upside to the current share price level.

To make the investment work, targeting a +15% IRR, the free cash flow margins need to be at the 55-56% level, which is not realistic at this point in time as the business is only in the initial phase of the recovery. Numbers of the analysis can be seen below.

If bearish: What could make the investment work?

Important Risks Involved

If the data center spending cools, this could affect future revenues and reverse the recent strong momentum.

Lattice's business model is to outsource manufacturing to TSMC, Samsung, and UMC, which gives them the “fabless” flexibility but ultimately makes them vulnerable to supply chain disruptions.

The stock is trading at a very rich valuation, which might suggest that all the good news about the future growth is already priced in.

The stock is part of a cyclical semiconductor industry dependent on industrial or automotive markets where inventories are still normalizing.

Investment Decision

At this point, I rate Lattice a Hold, as the risk-to-reward appears to be unfavorable and recovery is only at the early stage. We would need to see earnings catching up rapidly to justify such a valuation premium. I am happy to revisit my thinking after Q4 2025 results are published if the management guides us towards superior growth for the next year or net profit margins recover substantially towards historical 23-30% levels.

data.title: Joby Aviation: Buyers Are Back, Breaking Apart The Bearish Setup (Upgrade)
data.description:
I thought the bears were swirling for fodder as Joby Aviation, Inc. (JOBY) shares suffered an unceremonious bear market wave in the summer, but the wave of selling appears to have subsided. Incredibly, JOBY bottomed ahead of the $12.15 zone in November, which also nicely coincided with the trough back in September. In other words, dip buyers haven't allowed the selling fervor to take over decisively, as JOBY has maintained its ascent over the past four weeks, moving well past the drop zone in November.

I think Joby's Q3 earnings release has kept the eVTOL operator in the game for a more enterprising 2026 that could set the stage for its fledgling commercialization initiatives. Joby's pursuit of certification has reportedly progressed constructively, possibly having “FAA pilots to start for credit testing next year.” Notably, management has unveiled that it's in the final stages, helping to shape the bullish narrative in favor of long-term Joby investors. I'm upgrading my rating to a “Buy.”

Liquidity Through 2026 Shifts Focus Toward Commercialization Milestones

Why the focus on long-term? Well, for now, I don't think I need to demystify for you that Joby remains a long way from clawing its way out of the cash burn through the rest of the decade, although Wall Street is seemingly confident that Joby's journey to successful commercialization is a question of when, and not if. With that in mind, it should help the company scale its manufacturing, and capacity management, leveraging the fixed-cost economics that are inherent in its business model toward being a full-fledged operator, taking on domestic and global expansion opportunities at the same time.

While cash burn will likely remain the primary concern for investors contemplating taking a further look into Joby's thesis, it's notable that the company posted $978 million in cash and equivalents as of the third quarter, while expecting to consume cash close to the upper end of the $500 million to $540 million range.

Therefore, the company will need to continue raising financing through the rest of the decade while trying to validate the sustainability of its business model. Fortuitously, Joby has capitalized on its high valuation multiples to raise almost $580 million in net proceeds from equity financing. Hence, I believe buyers aren't perturbed with near-term cash flow risks based on the current cash burn estimates that should see it through 2026, redirecting the market's focus to its commercialization regime and momentum.

Back in my previous Joby write-up, I stated that while the commercialization progress is somewhat laudable, we must consider the long journey toward sustainable business economics. However, it seems like the assurances that the market received following Joby's third-quarter earnings call appear sufficient to bolster buying sentiments through this week.

Joby's Customers And Manufacturing Capabilities Have Likely Assured Investors

If you have been leaning close to Joby's recent updates, I believe you would probably concur the market is getting increasingly confident of its commercialization outcome. Needless to say, the previous surges that saw JOBY hit a zenith just under the $20 level show robust resistance and opportunistic profit-taking. Investors arguably are banking on an accelerated cadence toward full revenue service to validate its commercialization journey.

Back in the Q3 earnings call, discussion relating to Joby's “manufacturing processes” dominated the conference. Clearly, I believe Wall Street is sanguine enough to segue the discussion past the design phases, seeing that Joby is on the way to priming its TIA-ready aircraft, moving the company into the final certification stages.

Growth optionalities in defense and autonomy are interesting placeholders for now, which were also highlighted during the conference. However, I don't think the market will be overly concerned with the progress in these zones in the foreseeable future. Why?

Since Joby reportedly already has customers lining up, right, validating the underlying demand and quality of service delivery to justify Wall Street's medium-term prognosis. I'm in concurrence with management's confidence that the Blade deployment helps to kind of “derisk” the setup for Joby into subsequent commercial opportunities and validation (including domestic and global prospects). Management's point about Joby not “starting cold” is aligned with that perspective and promising thesis as compared to peers who may not quite have the same level of pedigree.

And then, it has accelerated its brand building in the Middle East, particularly in Dubai. These corroborate and pave the way for Joby into nascent overseas markets, which I believe augur well for the company's international push. These are positive customer proof points that could help elevate the company's credibility on future defense partnerships and collaborations, which I believe may not have been fully contemplated in JOBY's revenue outlook through 2029.

Buy Or Sell JOBY Stock?

To be brutally honest, I was surprised to discover that sellers didn't manage to muster a deeper selloff below JOBY's $12 support level that saw buyers flooding back to stem the falling tide.

It's a truly remarkable feat, highlighting that while the cash-burning company isn't expected to turn profitable anytime soon, the market isn't unduly worried about it. The focus has clearly been rechanneled toward Joby's commercialization cadence through 2026, which will likely determine whether the stock could mount another attempt to break out of the $20 zone eventually.

Even when we think about JOBY's FY2029 revenue multiple of 12.8x, it's still way above the industrials median of 1.8x. In other words, I believe buyers thinking about the setup right now must acknowledge that any such attempts should be construed within a highly speculative lens, as economics through 2029 are expected to stay palpably negative.

Still, going against the current recovering momentum seems like a folly for me to maintain. The buying advance is arguably unfolding nicely into a speculative opportunity for investors thinking about a 2026 commercialization milestone that should help keep buying sentiments steady.

Rating: Upgrade to speculative “Buy.”

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Market Reacts To Earnings
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Oracle's earnings showed mixed results, leading to an 11% drop (0:15). Broadcom's strong earnings overshadowed by warnings of margin pressures (2:30). AutoZone's miss highlights broader economic challenges (3:25). Airline relief rally following government shutdown (5:30). A streaming bidding war (7:05). Nike and FedEx earnings will provide insights into consumer spending (8:45). Explaining market uniformity and fluctuations between excitement for AI and concerns over costs (10:00). The Fed's uncertain policy, with multiple dissents (15:25).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, welcome back to another week of Wall Street Roundup.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here.

Rena Sherbill: Talk to us, what are you looking at this week?

Brian Stewart: I think the biggest headliner is Oracle (ORCL) dropped about 11% after its earnings report. Mixed results, beat on earnings, missed on revenue. Had been up seven consecutive sessions head into earnings. So to a certain extent, the 11% drop was just giving back the heightening expectations going into the earnings report. But if you pull back a little bit and compare this to the last earnings report, you kind of have a

A tale of two quarters going on. So in September, the company popped 36 % following its quarterly report. Then the main driver there was a huge rise in remaining performance obligations. This is a measure of future contracted revenue. At that point, the RPO was up 359 % hitting $455 billion. In this quarter, RPO was up 438%.

Bigger growth and reached $523 billion, so a larger overall number. So conceivably, investors should be pretty excited about this continuation of growth. But last time, stock was up 36%. This time it's down 11%. Also, it's been falling off since it popped in that September timeframe. It's been giving back a lot of that.

It's now down 40 % since that post earnings jump back in September, mostly worries about spending in debt. you dig into the results a little bit. looking at Oracle's cloud revenue was up 34 % in the most recent earnings report software revenue is down about 3%. But then if you look at expenses operating expenses for the cloud software segment, we're up 45%. So you see a continuation of the theme we've been seeing where there was a lot of.

AI related excitement around a lot of these companies in the wake of announcing new deals, getting the orders for the AI products that they're providing, but then a realization among investors of the costs associated with building out that infrastructure.

So there's a lot of worries around Oracle that it's taking on too much debt, its spending is rising faster than its revenues. And I think that's been holding the stock back after the earnings recently.

Rena Sherbill: We saw some earnings from Broadcom if we keep on with that sector. What did you see out of them?

Brian Stewart: Yeah, actually, Broadcom's (AVGO) a very similar story. It rose initially as earnings were released. It had pretty strong results and a strong forecast.

However, in the conference call following the earnings report, management warned of margin pressures. It attributed this to a higher mix of AI revenue. And now as we started recording, the stock is down about 10 % on Friday. So you have another situation where strong headline results are being undercut by worries about costs.

So I think this is going to be an ongoing theme as we see earnings come out, see whether or not these companies can actually get revenue to to match the costs are being put into building out the infrastructure for this.

One thing I'd to note is this isn't completely limited to the tech space. Another earnings report that came out this week was AutoZone (AZO). It fell 7 percent after its earnings. It missed expectations, even though Sandstorm sales were up 4.8 percent for the quarter that was below expectations and then margins were hurt by inflation, basically higher costs cutting into it.

AutoZone's a company that when you see cars getting too expensive, AutoZone's an auto parts retailer. When you see cars getting more expensive, you see people trying to keep their cars on the road longer, avoid buying new cars.

And so to a certain extent, you'd expect AutoZone to see a boost in murky economic times as people try to get the most out of, you know, whatever they got in the driveway now.

But you're seeing them unable to sort of capitalize on that situation because their costs are going up. And so I think this is a broad, even beyond AI, even behind tech, I think you're seeing a lot of companies struggle with the cost to revenue equation.

Rena Sherbill: And then if we stick with the transportation theme, we're looking at airlines. We've got some stocks there that have been up for consecutive days, up quite a nice amount.

And everybody knows the flying industry has been going fantastic. Everybody's experience in an airport, on an airline. I know everybody can join in chorus that it's been really something to mimic and imitate. So obviously those numbers are up, sarcasm included and hopefully acknowledged.

Brian, what would you say about Southwest (LUV), JetBlue (JBLU), United (UAL), all those stocks that are seeing some nice gains, Delta (DAL)?

Brian Stewart: A large chunk of it is just relief rally after the government shutdown ended. You see the the Jets ETF, which covers the airline sector, is up 14 % since the end of the shutdown. Basically, these stocks were going into Thanksgiving with a shutdown happening, the likelihood of having to cut back flights dramatically.

This is the key time for a lot of these airlines to book some revenue during the Hollywood Hollywood, they during the holiday travel part of the year. And so being able to resolve that before you hit the crisis point towards the of the year gave a huge boost to these. None more than Southwest among the larger airlines.

So it's 30 up 31 percent since the government shut down, its currently up for today, which would make its ninth straight day of gains. If it does finish higher today, it's going to be up 13 out of less 15 days. So you see a general meltup in the sector. Southwest, I think, is getting a boost from its general turnaround plans. I think that's why it's being rewarded as one of you know, spots that in the sector that people are putting money.

Basically Southwest becoming more of a quote unquote normal airline. They're changing their boarding pattern. There was a story earlier this week that they're thinking about having first class on some of their flights. So the idea of Southwest is sort of, know, everybody just kind of grabs whatever seat they can is changing.

And like you pointed out, the other airlines are also seeing pretty notable growth over the last few weeks. So JetBlue up 13 % since the end of the shutdown, United up 14%, American (AAL) up 15%, and then Delta is outpacing that little group by being up 22%.

So there are places in the market, you know, with Oracle down, with the general AI sell off think there's a little bit of worry, kind of gloom and doom, but there are pockets where investors are finding places to make

Rena Sherbill: And is now the time that we talk about Netflix (NFLX) and Warner Brothers (WBD) and Paramount (PSKY) and a bidding war. We're in a bidding war.

Brian Stewart: I feel like we're going to be talking about this on and off for a while now.

Rena Sherbill: Fingers crossed, Brian, fingers crossed.

Brian Stewart: Indications are this is good. Indications are this is going to stretch into 2026. You've got the bidding war itself going on, whether Netflix is going to win or Paramount's going to win. And then you have the eventual. Antitrust discussions, whoever buys it, I think.

The feeling now is that the Paramount bit is a little more concerning in terms of antitrust, but then, you know, there's the feeling that the paramount is closer to the administration. So maybe that gives them a little easier time. So those kind of considerations are going to play out over time. I think in terms of the industry in general, I think this is a sign that the content remains king.

And even there's been a big push for the streaming services to get more into live events. So you see things like boxing on Netflix and you see, you know, football on various streamers and things like that. I do think that the desire for these companies to get the Warner Brothers assets, the studio and the library, I think it's a sign that there's still some room for more traditional movies, TV shows, things of that nature.

We also see Disney (DIS) up recently. Disney had a deal with the OpenAI (OPENAI). So it's another content focused company that's been getting some attention lately. So I think you're seeing a resurgence in interest in the more traditional content producer.

Rena Sherbill: And earnings wise, theme wise, what are you looking at next week, stock wise?

Brian Stewart: So as we head into the holidays, the pace of earnings is starting to turn into a dribble. But there are also some interesting stuff coming out. Nike (NKE) coming out next week. That's going to give a good indication of consumer spending habits. So look for the commentary there.

Another key one is going to be FedEx (FDX), especially as we start moving into the holiday shipping season, or more than move into like we're at the height of it now. So you're to get some commentary from them about how things look. So that's that's going to be an early holiday shopping signal will be what FedEx has to say about that.

And then there's a couple of homebuilders, Lennar (LEN) and KB Home (KBH) reporting. So that's another key part of the economy that I feel has fallen by the wayside. I mean, part of the whole affordability discussion that's been going on is the high price of homes and the fact that you have to be a 50 year old software engineer to afford a house these days.

I feel like overall the housing industry in terms of the stock market has been greatly overshadowed by tech. And so it'll be interesting to kind of see what's the conditions of that market.

Rena Sherbill: And anything to say about tech trading so uniformly? Any more insight to provide there? We've seen it so much, any words to give to investors about that?

Brian Stewart: Yeah, I feel like some of these swings might feel little arbitrary to investors as you're looking at the market in more casual way.

And I'm especially thinking about what we were talking about the Oracle situation where sort of similar results got a huge pop in September and a decline in December when the next earnings come out.

This kind of uniformity that you're seeing, I think it's just the way investors are metabolizing these results. I'm trying to think of a nice word for group think, but there's kind of a broad consensus that has been shifting between excitement for the prospects for AI and then an understanding that there's, you have to kind of buy those, buy that opportunity with the build out of the infrastructure here.

And I think in the early stages of that, people were sort of excited about the build out because it meant huge growth at places like Nvidia. But then when you start moving down the line, somebody's got to pay for that. So you see the Oracles, you see the Metas (META), you see a lot of these companies that are footing the bill for all these, the AI infrastructure that's being built out. And so I just feel like investors sometimes bounce back and forth between that excitement and the concerns that maybe that growth horizon is further out than we expect.

Rena Sherbill: It seems very similar to me to the cannabis industry and also the human nature of building something up only to later take it down, which includes a fierce amount of hope and build up to your point, which is not necessarily, and I think it seems to me now oftentimes not, predicated on a lot of hard numbers, but mostly a lot of speculation and promises of expected growth that does not always come to pass.

Speaking of cannabis, as we're looking at this rescheduling announcement that may be coming from Trump next week, which has been bandied about for so long. So to your point about groupthink and what that brings to the markets, it seems a pretty strong case of that.

Brian Stewart: I mean, the cannabis comparison is interesting. A little different in the fact that the cannabis situation is really kind of a regulatory concern, which in a way is less rational. Yeah, then the market conditions for AI, at least those can be sort of predicted in a meaningful way.

Not that anyone ever gets them exactly right, but still, you're dealing with laws of economics rather than just whatever goes on in Congress behind closed doors.

Rena Sherbill: But if I may, I would also add though that tech, at this point especially, is definitely influenced by regulatory pressures and things of that nature. I mean, China and Nvidia (NVDA) and the chips and all of those different factors, I think also a big part of things and how much jockeying tech is doing in government.

Brian Stewart: Yeah, that's an excellent point, especially the foreign trade aspect of it. I was thinking about more in terms of the situation where Nvidia doesn't have to worry that it can't open a bank account in certain states, but yeah, I think that's an excellent point.

Rena Sherbill: For sure. There are differences, for sure.

Brian Stewart: That you have these you have an endpoint, an investor has an endpoint in their mind of where they think this this company is going to end up. But you get so focused on that far horizon that you don't see the obstacles in front of you. You have to crawl through a swamp or over a mountain to sort of get to that point far off in the horizon.

And so I think there's a sort of a reality that you take that step in the swamp and, know, for the first time your foot gets wet that you start to think, wow, is this worth the journey? And so I think that's sort of the complication that investors are struggling with.

And that's why I think you'll see these waves of excitement. And I think some of the like you were talking about the sort of group thinker, the consensus investing, I think a lot of it is kind of a FOMO when stocks go up, you don't want to be the person left behind when the AI train really hits high gear. But then you don't want to be the last one holding the bag if there's going to be a bubble popping.

So I think that the very fact that there's so many conceivable outcomes, at least in the near term, you're talking, you know, 2026, 2027. People are speculating anything from a new wave of growth for AI is all the infrastructure is laid and now we can finally just hit the gas on bringing in these revenues to this bubble is going to burst. And the entire economy is going to get sunk by the money that's been wasted on AI.

And so when you're dealing with those two different possibilities and everything in between, I think it makes sense that investors might bounce around a little bit in their judgment.

Rena Sherbill: Hashtag human nature, hashtag market sentiment. Speaking of group think and dissenting from group think, we've got the Federal Reserve meeting, which we've talked a bunch about this week on this very podcast and elsewhere on Seeking Alpha.

What would you contextualize for the people when it comes to that meeting? A lot was expected, but the dissension is growing.

Brian Stewart: Absolutely.

I think this is one of the most uncertain times in terms of Fed policy that I can remember, except during sort of an emergency situation like COVID. But even in a situation like COVID, you can kind of predict what the Fed's going to do.

But in this situation, we had three dissents for the vote that eventually cut rates. And of those dissents, two wanted no cut and one wanted a 50 basis point cut. So even among the dissents, there's not a consensus of where it's not like there's two camps in the Fed kind fighting it out. have, you it's not even three, you you've multiple people with multiple visions of what's going to happen in the near future.

You kind of land on a consensus, but I think it's papering over. And I think it makes it difficult for investors to kind of predict where interest rates are going to go. The general consensus now is that interest rates can be relatively stable in 2026, the dot plot that came along with the the Fed announcement.

The consensus was for one cut in 2026. That's roughly in line with what markets are saying. Markets are saying basically a 32 percent chance of two cuts in 2026, 24 percent chance of one cut. So basically between 25 basis points and 75 basis points lower at some point in 2026.

You also see inflation staying kind of stubbornly above the 2 % target in 2026 and maybe coming back to where people want to be in the 2027, 2028 range. So you're still looking two years, maybe three years to kind of win the inflation fight that's happening.

But you have an economic situation where the perception seems to be within the Fed that the economy's too fragile to keep rates high for any meaningful period of time. So, you know, you're just going to have to win a war of attrition against inflation, which is difficult for consumers who are already worried about affordability, who are already starting to put back their spending.

That's the conditions that are coming into play. And then next week, we're going to start getting the up to date, more current economic data. So we're going to finally get the November jobs report in the middle of the week next week. And we're going to get the CPI report in the middle of the week next week.

So we're going to start to start to catch up to where we were in terms of incoming data before the government shutdown. So that'll give a little bit better real time information. I mean, it's always a little backward looking, but more real time than it has been.

As we go into the holiday season, you're gonna see volume drop, you're gonna see maybe room for more swing. Quiet on news, but maybe a little jumpy in terms of stock movement.

data.title: Eos Energy Enterprises: High-Risk, High-Reward Bet On The Zinc-Bromine Battery
data.description:
Investment Thesis

EOS Energy Enterprises (EOSE) is a rapidly growing business that is poised to be able to take advantage of the AI power boom and America’s drive for energy independence. These factors make me bullish on the company despite the fact that it is currently in a phase where it is having to burn cash in order to build the capacity needed to be profitable. In addition to its positioning, the company has demonstrated remarkable operational success, where it is able to deliver strong growth in its quarterly data.

Strategic Overview Of The Company

One of the aspects of EOSE that makes it stand out in the energy sector is that it utilizes zinc-bromine to offer a non-flammable option compared to lithium-ion batteries. This has the potential to help reduce reliance on rare earth materials from China while also achieving similar performance. As an example, its Z3 product line has shown efficiency of up to 89% on less than 4-hour charge cycle.

In addition to the company’s continued work on improving the efficiency of its batteries, it is also working aggressively to ramp up its production capabilities. The company is currently working to expand its production rate to 2 GWh per year. As part of the company’s efforts to scale, it has entered into a deal with Talen Energy for a production facility that is optimized for scalability.

Lastly, the company has undergone a management shakeup that is designed to help focus it on scaling operations. I believe this is best shown by the appointment of John Mahaz as Chief Operating Officer. He has been tasked with leading operations, supply chain development, and manufacturing strategy. Top talent is necessary for the company to be able to navigate this transitory period.

EOSE’s Strengths Versus Its Sector Peers

EOSE offers a number of advantages compared to its peers, and I see this as being a major source of the market’s bullish sentiment towards the company. One of the most important advantages is the ability of the company’s product to reduce the fire risks that are a major hazard with lithium-ion batteries.

Additionally, its products are manufactured domestically, which helps to shield the company from some of the rare earth mineral disputes that are currently ongoing with China. As a result, the company is able to enjoy better supply chain security. Furthermore, the company benefits from $90 million per year in 45X production credits for manufacturing at full capacity. This is a sizable advantage that should not be quickly overlooked by investors that are curious about the company.

EOSE Overview: A Company Transitioning

When discussing Eos Energy, it is important to note that the company is difficult to evaluate in traditional metrics. When looking at the raw numbers and traditional metrics, it presents itself as a high-risk but high-reward option. For example, the company has an “A” grade in growth but an “F” in profitability; it's a classic scaling story. This makes it clear to me that this business is in a sprint to market dominance before it achieves maturity.

In terms of revenue, the company is almost ideal for a growth investor. The company’s revenue base is modest at $63.5 million, but it has a radical growth velocity of 324%. This is an explosion in growth, and the market seems to be largely supporting the acceleration. There is a projection of forward revenue growth of 205%; some estimates expect the company to hit over $1.6 billion by the end of 2028. I see this as clearly being one of the most important drivers in the stock’s current valuation.

Despite the growth, there is a reality check in terms of profitability. For example, the company has a profitability grade of “F”, and this is well earned. It has a gross profit margin of -177.94%. In other words, every dollar of revenue the company generates, it loses almost two. This is a serious operational loss that flows through the income statement. As a result, it has a staggering operational net income loss of $1.11 billion. Therefore, the company’s bull case rests entirely on its ability to drive down its cost of revenue relative to total revenue so that it can move its gross profit margin to positive territory.

A review of the cash flow statement confirms the capital intensity of the company’s ramp-up. The levered free cash flow is negative -$126.8 million; this burn rate requires constant access to capital. This forces the company to take on significant debt to the tune of a total debt level of $451 million. For the company to survive, it must be able to transition from a cash burn to cash positive. Unfortunately, this may not happen until the year 2027, when it has a consensus EPS estimate of $0.40. These factors are the hallmark of a company that is high-growth but still pre-profit, making it a race against the clock.

Assigning A Value To EOSE

Valuing EOSE comes with challenges, as it is a high-growth company with a speculative profit profile and a high cash-burn rate. The stock currently holds a valuation grade of “C+”. Due to the company being so deep in the red, traditional valuation metrics like current or trailing P/E ratios will give us results that are not particularly meaningful. Therefore, I will have to use methods that are more focused on the company’s future rather than its present. This is what the market appears to be doing as it currently has a trailing price-to-sales of 61.79 compared to the sector’s median of 1.80. I believe this multiple underscores the market’s expectations that the company will be successful in its efforts to massively scale up.

Due to the absence of current profitability, I will use a forward price-to-earnings model based on future projects in order to estimate a potential price for the stock. For these results, I will assume that EOSE can be at least moderately successful in its ambitious manufacturing and sales growth. To this end, I assume the consensus EPS estimate of $1.52 for 2028 is valid.

To choose a multiple, I will assume that the market rewards the company but that it still slightly lags the overall sector median of 21.29. A multiple of 18 seems reasonable, as it is slightly conservative while still holding to the company’s explosive growth. With these assumptions in place, I can multiply them together to generate a potential price of $27.36. This is much higher than its current trading price and shows that there is sizable upside to this company.

Risks To The Bullish Outlook

Capital Needs

One of the primary weaknesses of the company as it currently stands is its intense need for sustained capital to keep expanding. In addition to being a direct threat to the company, I also see this as a risk of dilution for shareholders. An example of this is the company’s direct equity offering and a private offering of convertible senior notes. While this helps to raise the capital the company needs, it does pose a threat of dilution that I do not think should be ignored. Furthermore, the company filed to sell over 7 million shares of common stock related to warrants, and this is a clear dilution event.

Highly Volatile Earnings

Another troubling sign for the company is that it appears to be developing a history of missing consensus estimates. A recent example of this is the company missing its third quarter estimates by over 9 million. Furthermore, it also suffered a significant miss during the second quarter. While this is not unusual for companies that are in the same phase of EOSE, it is still a troubling trend for a company that is relying on its ability for rapid growth in order to sustain its valuation. Management’s efforts to continue ramping up while also cutting costs can help to offset these issues for the company as it moves forward.

Backlog Issues

EOSE is facing some issues in terms of the timing of customer financing, along with its project execution. While management’s tone has shifted to be more optimistic in terms of its current backlog, this is still another issue that will require the company to walk a tightrope to be able to effectively handle. Otherwise, it could start to lose the confidence of customers, which could greatly slow its potential growth while also allowing competitors to close the gap on the company’s new battery technology.

Conclusion

I see Eos Energy Enterprises as an exciting but challenging company. Investments in it must be based on the belief in the company’s future ability to execute rather than its current performance. This is not unusual for a company that is in a high-growth phase in an infrastructure-heavy space, such as building zinc-bromine batteries. Much of the bullish case rests on the company’s future aligning with the AI power boom and moves to energy independence by the US. However, if the company is able to achieve its growth targets, I see it as potentially reaching $27.36, which shows that there is major upside for the company moving forward. While still a speculative investment, I view the company as bullish and rate it as a Buy for investors that are willing to tolerate the risk of a speculative investment in their portfolio.

data.title: ICF: Providing Passive Exposure To An Industry Diversified Basket Of REITs
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The iShares Select U.S. REIT ETF (ICF) provides investors with passive exposure to a broad basket of real estate investment trust holdings. The index is based on the Cohen & Steers Realty Majors Index—Cohen & Steers (CNS) is a well-known investment manager with a specific focus on real estate investments.

With that said, and as we have previously noted, the index that's constructed hasn't delivered a significant amount of alpha relative to the other real estate-focused ETFs available for investors. In fact, over the longer term, it has notably fallen short relative to the other choices available to investors.

ICF Basics

  • Dividend Frequency: Quarterly
  • Dividend Yield: 2.55% (SEC yield 2.68%)
  • Expense Ratio: 0.32%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $1.881 billion
  • Structure: Passive ETF

ICF's investment objective is to "track the investment results of an index composed of U.S. real estate investment trusts." Providing exposure to "large real estate companies that are dominant in their respective property sectors."

We covered a deeper outline in our initial coverage of this fund, and the full report can be read, but we'll highlight the major points.

  • First, and probably it goes without saying, it has to be a REIT.
  • The market cap has to be over $500 million.
  • Average trading volume needs to be at least 600k shares for six months.
  • Then the active part of the CNS comes in as they select the 30 they believe "best represent the Realty Majors."

After that, they rank holdings by market cap, but no position can be over an 8% weighting. The fund rebalances on the third Friday of February, May, August, and November.

This fund pays a quarterly distribution. Similar to most ETFs, it is based on what it receives in dividends paid from the underlying holdings, and that is passed on to investors. That means the payout changes every quarter, and as we've seen for the last several years now, that can be quite drastic differences from quarter to quarter.

Performance Comparison

Since our last update, the fund has delivered slightly positive total returns—but certainly nothing to boast about, as it significantly underperformed the S&P 500 Index.

Looking at more appropriate direct comparisons in the REIT space and we see that again, the fund really can't boast about anything.

Over the longer term, ICF has underperformed the State Street Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLRE) by a wide margin. The fund has also fallen quite short relative to the other real estate-focused iShares offering, the iShares US Real Estate ETF (IYR). Against the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), it has been able to put up a bit more respectable numbers.

In looking back at the last five years, we have an interesting period of time that was particularly rough on REITs, the very aggressive interest rate hikes we saw from the Fed, and the rising long-term risk-free yields. Both of which negatively impact REITs.

First, higher yields mean safer alternatives can start to look better as a source of income for investors, who can then offload their riskier REIT holdings.

Second, REITs utilize a high level of leverage; rising rates mean their borrowing costs will be subject to higher interest rates as well.

With that, a look at the last five years for all of these ETFs shows relatively low returns, but interestingly, ICF does start to look more respectable.

For passively managed index-tracking ETFs, it can sometimes come down to something as simple as higher or lower expense ratios. For ICF, the expense ratio comes in at 32 basis points; XLRE is at a razor-thin 8 bps, with VNQ at 13 bps and IYR at 38 bps. So that is playing some of a factor, but clearly isn't the only thing going on here for the fund over the longer term, as the underperformance was quite significant.

ICF Portfolio Comparison

The fund is fairly diversified across different REITs focused on various industries. However, the fund is also fairly concentrated in several of the top holdings, which can take some of the benefits of being truly diversified away.

Welltower (WELL), Prologis REIT (PLD) and American Tower REIT (AMT) all take commanding weightings at over 8%, resulting in those three positions alone being ~25% of the fund's total investments.

Still, each does belong to a very distinct underlying industry that is very different from the other: healthcare, industrial, and cell tower, respectively.

With that, given the market-cap-weighted ranking that is popular among many of the passively managed index funds, we will see some significant overlap with its peers.

In the case of ICF vs. XLRE, we have 83% overlap with 25 holdings. ICF holdings only 30 positions, with XLRE at 31.

In looking toward the top holdings, we can see exactly what we expected to see. WELL, PLD and AMT are the largest positions, followed by Equinix (EQIX) and Simon Property Group (SPG).

With ICF vs. VNQ, we see the percentage overlap being quite a bit lower. However, VNQ holdings 160 positions—which actually results in holding all 30 of the same holdings of ICF. Therefore, the actual overlap is reduced due to the sheer number of other holdings.

Despite the meaningfully greater number of holdings, looking at the top holdings, we see once again significant overlap. The weightings for WELL and PLD are almost identical.

In the case of ICF and IYR, we similarly see that the percentage overlap is lower when compared to XLRE, but again, it is due to the higher number of holdings for IYR at 61 holdings.

For the top holdings, we can see a slight difference here, in that SPG is a larger holding than AMT for IYR. However, that's quite a minor difference.

With that, despite the longer-term showing ICF as a weaker performer, the significant overlap that we've seen from the holdings could suggest that we'll see much closer performance at this time. That's consistent with what we've been seeing for the last five years, so that looks set to continue.

Conclusion

Over the long term, ICF has underperformed its passively managed ETF peers, which is disappointing to see from an index modeled by CNS, a niche asset manager in the real estate space. However, over the last five years, during a period that was overall having quite significant headwinds for REITs, the fund has started to perform much more respectably. Given the overlap of these peer funds today, similar performance would be expected to continue as well. That doesn't give ICF too much of an edge, but with a lower rate environment as the Fed continues its cutting cycle, that should bode well for REITs more broadly.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Zscaler Has Fallen But The Neighborhood Is On Sale
data.description:
Sometimes patience can be the hardest thing in investing. Zscaler (ZS) has pulled back significantly in the past quarter. Those who avoided the stock during the summer months may be viewing the recent weakness as a potential buying opportunity. I urge further patience, as one can find no shortage of even cheaper names in the software space. The stock may look more buyable than earlier in the year, but it is a buyer’s market in the software space. I reiterate my neutral rating for the stock.

ZS Stock Price

I last covered ZS in October, where I cautioned on the valuation. The stock has fallen 23% since.

The valuation contraction has helped the valuation proposition, but I should note the greater slide seen in the broader software space.

ZS Stock Key Metrics

ZS is a cybersecurity enterprise software company. It is disrupting the traditional firewall product with its “zero trust” protocol. The easy way to think about it is that whereas legacy firewalls protect access to the network, ZS allows its customers to customize security access on an app-by-app basis. This is far superior and is what’s needed in the cloud economy.

The company has also been a beneficiary of the rise of AI, as it has introduced products to secure access to AI. I expect these products to gain more traction as AI further makes its way through society.

In the most recent quarter, the company generated 26% YoY revenue growth to $788 million, crushing guidance of between $772 million to $774 million.

The generated $172 million in non-GAAP operating profits, exceeding guidance of between $166 million to $168 million and representing around 100 bps of margin expansion.

The company did not make progress towards GAAP profitability, but I am not concerned, as this is the kind of business in which I can see management ramping up margins if and when they decide to do so.

ZS ended the quarter with $3.3 billion of cash and investments versus $1.7 billion in convertible notes, representing a strong net cash balance sheet. I note that using convertible notes has the benefit of lower interest expenses.

Looking ahead, management guided the second quarter to see around 23% YoY revenue growth to between $797 million and $799 million. Management raised the full-year guide to up to $3.3 billion, up from the prior guide of between $3.265 billion to $3.284 billion.

On the conference call, management noted that they “expect AI Security ARR to exceed $0.5 billion by the end of this fiscal year.” This is a product in which I can see growth rates remaining top tier for a long time, as the rise of AI agents may lead to ever-increasing demand for cybersecurity. In the near term, management noted that the ongoing migration of on-premise SAP software (SAP) to their cloud RISE product is expected to drive solid tailwinds due to customers having the opportunity to add cybersecurity cloud products in the process.

Is ZS Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At recent prices, ZS traded at around 12x this year’s sales and 10x 2027e sales. Consensus estimates call for a very modest pace of decelerating top-line growth over the coming years.

The company is expected to deliver solid operating leverage, culminating in around 25% net margins by 2032.

Courtesy of significant weakness in the software sector, ZS is not trading remotely close to bubbly valuations. I expect the company to see top-line growth decelerate more meaningfully than consensus, but even then, the valuation looks more reasonable than in the recent past. Assuming the company can achieve 30% net margins over the long term, that places the stock at around 33x 2027e long-term earnings. Against 15% to 19% revenue growth, that represents around a 2x price-to-earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’), which is reasonable given the company’s high-quality business model. In normal times this would justify a "Buy" rating, but the software sector has significantly underperformed as of late. I’d also note that this valuation multiple looks rather “full” when compared against the mega-cap tech companies like Microsoft (MSFT) or Alphabet (GOOGL) which trade at comparable or lower price-to-earnings multiples.

I am seeing many software names in my coverage universe trading at far lower valuations, even on a growth-adjusted basis. For instance, Salesforce (CRM) trades at around 5x forward sales, with its slower growth rate being offset by high GAAP margins. I find it prudent to remain patient and wait for the stock to trade at around $215 per share, which would represent a 10.5x 2026e and 8.8x 2027e sales multiple.

ZS Stock Conclusion

The market may be too pessimistic about the company’s prospects in the near term, as ZS may see some tailwinds from the RISE migration. That said, the stock still trades at an outsized premium to software peers, and the magnitude of that premium is difficult to ignore. ZS has many of the traits I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio, but strategically speaking, this looks like a buyer’s market in the software sector. I continue to recommend patience in waiting for even more attractive valuations.

data.title: UnitedHealth's 2026 Could Surprise The Street
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) stock is down by over 33% YTD, and I can't say that this massive dip was unjustified. The company's insurance businesses have been facing pressure due to rising medical cost trends, and the medical loss ratio (MLR) has been quite elevated versus the historical norms because of increased senior healthcare utilization. Despite all that, I first called UNH a "Buy" when it was down 43% YTD in mid-May 2025. It looks like I almost caught the local bottom of the price action because, since that call of mine, UNH is up by ~25% (in total returns). And the stock has been rising and beating the market with my every update.

It might look controversial - if the business remains in deterioration, why am I calling the stock a "Buy" then? But I think every dip has its limit, and what matters in such cases is the way the management behaves. I like what I see in UNH's strategic moves lately, and their more conservative approach to drive up the margins. I think that next year, UNH is likely to achieve better-than-expected margins and hence EPS, so that it can restart buybacks and support the stock price. UNH is undervalued in this regard, so I keep a bullish eye on the upcoming 2026.

Why Do I Think So?

My most recent article on UNH came out in August, so I physically couldn't cover the firm's Q3 2025 results. All that is already reflected in the price, but let me briefly take a look at what has happened during the last quarter.

UNH reported ~$113.16 billion in revenues (+12.24% YoY), slightly beating the consensus estimate (I'd call it "in-line reporting"). On the bottom line, UNH's adjusted EPS reached $2.92 (-59% YoY), and while the YoY dynamic looked ugly, the EPS figure itself beat the consensus estimate by ~4%, according to Seeking Alpha. So, effectively, UNH came back to its historical beating potential after two deep misses in Q1 and Q2 2025:

We saw that their medical loss ratio was ~89.9% in Q3 (vs. 85.2% last year), which is exactly the trend that explains why the stock fell so aggressively earlier in 2025. But the management framed the Q3 MLR as "historically high but consistent", likely meaning that Q3 business/industry conditions didn't introduce a new risk to MLR's upside in the future quarters.

For the current year, overall UnitedHealthcare performance remains in line with the expectations we offered in the second quarter. Medical cost trends remained historically high but consistent with our second quarter guidance, and we expect that to continue throughout the remainder of 2025.

Source: CEO's comments on the Q3 earnings call

The new management has been shrinking uneconomic exposure to reverse the margins trend. In particular, now they expect ~1million Medicare Advantage membership contraction in 2026, with a big piece of it coming from plan exits impacting ~600,000 members (plus additional disciplined attrition in group and individual MA). It's a big step toward margin normalization because MA has been a low-single-digit margin business where a small amount of mispricing (or an unusually hot utilization trend) can harm profitability for an entire year. So, by getting rid of those one million members, UNH should effectively cut out the worst-performing cohorts within its sales mix, and so the margins will likely naturally go up throughout 2026 because lower loss-making exposure means better margin-per-member.

I know that the member reduction might seem like a harsh hit to the top-line, but we should remember that UNH is operating with a very large base, having a domestic membership of over 50 million, which was up by 750,000 this year alone (on a YTD basis). And the membership reductions are being paired with explicit pricing resets that support revenue per member:

Moving to ACA markets. We have submitted rate filings in nearly all of the 30 states where we participate that reflect 2025 morbidity and experience. These include average rate increases of over 25%.

Trends for 2025 and our outlook for 2026 remain in line with what we shared in the guidance last quarter. Trends are approximately 11%, and that is how we have priced into 2026, and I'll just share that. With 50% of our January insured business resolved at this point, encouraged by both the yield on persistency and rate to deliver the margin expansion that Tim spoke about in light of that trend of approximately 11%.

Source: UNH's earnings call, the CEO's and CSGO's commentary, emphasis added by the author

So, when the clean-up year ends (I'm talking about FY2026), UNH's top-line is likely to revive because the demographics of an aging population remain a favorable tailwind.

The number of Americans ages 65 and older is projected to increase from 58 million in 2022 to 82 million by 2050 (a 42% increase), and the 65-and-older age group’s share of the total population is projected to rise from 17% to 23%.

Source: pbr.org

The increasing population of older Americans will need health benefit plans and services to manage their healthcare needs. UNH is still the largest publicly traded managed care company by revenue, and it's set to be one of the main beneficiaries of that secular trend. And we see that in FY2027, UNH's revenue growth comes back up above 4% on a YoY basis after a muted FY2026 trend:

What's even more important, all the strategic moves the management makes now to revive margins should translate to a reasonable recovery in EPS already next year, and UNH is also expected to show a 17.33% YoY rise in net earnings in FY2027:

I truly believe that given the company's scale and role in the US market, it should have a valuation premium over its peers. And even compared to historical P/E ratios, we see that on FY2025 EPS, the stock looks undervalued by over 10%.

I'd assume that the management's plan goes the way it's laid out, and the current consensus is overly pessimistic by the average beating size of the past years. In other words, I assume that UNH can beat the current EPS consensus by 7-8% in FY2026. If they beat Q4 2025, and then beat Q1 and Q2 2026 by just a few percentage points, there would come positive revisions, leaving the current FY2026 EPS consensus in the dust. So, my core assumption looks reasonable to me.

Anyway, assuming that UNH comes back up to its 10-year average P/E ratio by the end of 2026, we'd have a price target of $437.70 per share:

17.75 * 1.075 * 22.94x = $437.70/share

This gives me an upside potential of ~30%, and that's why I remain bullish on UNH even after its off-bottom recovery of ~42% so far this year.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

I think that the main risk to my thesis today is if the medical cost trend simply refuses to cool. The Q3 MLR is indeed too high, and it needs to go down by 9-11% just to match the historical norms. So, if the system remains in a senior utilization upcycle, then even a rational plan to revive margins could still lead to choppy earnings ahead. My assumptions for FY2026/2027 EPS growth rates would become too optimistic and misleading, and the stock wouldn't reach the P/E level I'm expecting.

The Bottom Line

Despite the risks, I don't think that UNH actually needs a heroic macro tailwind to show a meaningful recovery from here. The business' stress is visible to everyone, and that elevated MLR has already led to an ugly price action before. In Q3, when the MLR was also red hot, the stock price didn't crash as hard as we've seen before. Yes, it dropped by 16% throughout a few sessions, but instead of continuation, the price recovered, and UNH is down by only 8% since the Q3 print. Assuming that UNH delivers modestly better-than-feared margins as the worst cohorts roll off, I believe the next phase would be upward revisions for EPS figures (for both FY2026 and FY2027). If so, a multiple expansion would follow, and UNH could rise by 30% from here if my valuation model plays out. Based on all that, I remain bullish on UNH stock.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: JCPB: I Prefer JPIE's Risk-Benefit Profile
data.description:
This article updates my review of March 2025 in light of recent performance and current portfolio.

JCPB strategy

JPMorgan Core Plus Bond ETF (JCPB) is an actively managed bond ETF launched on 01/28/2019. JCPB has 2,571 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 4.89%, a net yield to maturity of 4.91% and a net expense ratio of 0.38%. Distributions are paid monthly. JPMorgan offers a similar strategy in a mutual fund (HLIPX).

As described in the prospectus by JPMorgan Asset Management, the fund may invest in corporate bonds, U.S. government and agency securities, asset-backed and mortgage-backed securities, unrated and sub-investment grade debt securities, including from international markets. Under normal conditions, the fund enforces maximum limits of:

  • 30% of assets in sub-investment grade debt (“junk bonds”);
  • 10% of assets in sub-prime mortgage-related securities;
  • 25% of assets in international securities.

The fund may also use derivatives for investing and hedging purposes. The portfolio turnover rate was 111% in the most recent fiscal year and 57% in the previous year.

Portfolio

As of writing, the portfolio is mostly invested in securities of U.S. issuers (90.5% of asset value), with significant exposure in treasuries/futures (31.1%), agency mortgage-backed securities (27%) and investment grade corporate bonds (23.6%). No other debt category weighs more than 10%.

Securities with an investment grade (BBB rating or higher) weigh 78.7% of assets, with 47% rated AAA.

The portfolio’s average remaining maturity is 8.35 years. As for interest rate risk, the average duration is 6.08 years.

I won’t list the top 10 holdings here: a single issuer may have many securities in the 2,500+ holdings, and JPMorgan doesn’t provide issuer weights. The sector breakdown suggests that the largest issuers are U.S. Treasury, Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac and Ginnie Mae.

Performance

Since 2/4/2019, JCPB has:

  • outperformed a total bond market benchmark (BND) and a Treasury bond benchmark (GOVT), with similar volatility and a shallower maximum drawdown;
  • almost tied with a corporate investment grade benchmark (LQD), with lower risk in drawdown and volatility;
  • underperformed a high yield bond benchmark (HYG), with lower risk.

Data: Portfolio123.

The share price has lost 6.4% since inception in January 2019, while cumulative inflation was about 29% (based on CPI).

The annual sum of distributions has increased from $1.66 to $2.38 per share between 2020 and 2024, despite a fall in 2021, as plotted on the next chart. This 4-year dividend growth of 43% is closely related to interest rates trend (second chart below) and should not be projected as a long-term expectation. Nonetheless, it has nicely outpaced inflation.

JCPB vs. competitors

The next table compares characteristics of JCPB and five actively managed bond ETFs with yields close to 5% and at least three years of track record:

  • JPMorgan Income ETF (JPIE).
  • Dimensional Core Fixed Income ETF (DFCF).
  • PIMCO Active Bond Exchange-Traded Fund (BOND).
  • Capital Group Core Plus Income ETF (CGCP).
  • State Street® DoubleLine® Total Return Tactical ETF (TOTL).

This list isn’t intended to be exhaustive.
